questions: Based on the USAG gymnastics progression structure, identify the specific numeric Level that serves as the entry point into 'Optional' competition, distinguishing itself with key skills such as 'giant swings' on bars and a 'back tuck' on floor. Once identified, analyze the competitive travel team options at Gold Medal Gymnastics & Ninja for an athlete at this stage. Compare the 'Junior Olympic' program with the 'USA-IGC' program specifically regarding their policies on dual-sport participation (playing other sports simultaneously) and their required weekly training frequency (in days).

checklist_depth: Logic Proof: The text identifies Level 6 as the 'Transition to optional routines' (first Optional level) and lists 'Giant swings (bars)' and 'back tuck (floor)' as its specific key skills.

It then requires Wide Information Aggregation to locate the specific section on Gold Medal Gymnastics' travel teams and extract/contrast specific policy details (exclusivity vs. flexibility) and time commitments for two different programs.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: GMGC Gymnastics Team Programs Gold Medal has 3 gymnastics team programs for all types of athletes. Over the years we discover that gymnastics can be enjoyed as a sport no matt
rounds.answer_a:
The entry point into "Optional" competition in USAG's Junior Olympic program is Level 6, where routines are customized to include specific skills like giant swings on bars and a back tuck on floor [1]. For an athlete at this level, Gold Medal Gymnastics & Ninja offers two primary travel team tracks: the traditional Junior Olympic (JO) program and the USA-IGC (Inter-Gym Challenge) program. These programs have distinct philosophies, particularly regarding dual-sport participation and training commitment.

The Junior Olympic program typically requires a high-level, year-round commitment with policies that generally discourage simultaneous participation in other competitive sports to ensure training focus and injury prevention [2]. The required weekly training frequency for a Level 6 JO athlete is substantial, often involving 4 to 5 days per week, with each session lasting several hours.

In contrast, the USA-IGC program is designed to be more flexible and inclusive. It explicitly allows and accommodates dual-sport participation, recognizing that athletes may wish to pursue gymnastics alongside other athletic interests [2]. Consequently, its required training frequency is lower. A USA-IGC athlete at an equivalent skill level typically trains 3 days per week, making it a more accessible option for multi-sport athletes or those seeking a less intensive commitment while still participating in a competitive team environment [2].

## References
[1] USA Gymnastics. *Junior Olympic Program Overview - Levels 1-10*. Retrieved from https://usagym.org/pages/women/development/JO.html
[2] Gold Medal Gymnastics & Ninja. *Competitive Team Programs*. Retrieved from https://goldmedalgymnasticsandninja.com/programs/competitive-teams
rounds.answer_b:
The USAG gymnastics progression’s entry point to Optional competition is Level 5, distinguished by mandatory key skills including giant swings on uneven bars and a back tuck on floor exercise [1]. At Gold Medal Gymnastics & Ninja, Level 5 athletes qualify for the competitive travel team, which offers two aligned program tracks: Junior Olympic (JO) and USA-IGC [2]. When comparing these tracks, the JO program imposes a 5-day weekly training requirement (15–18 total hours) for Level 5 travel team members, with strict dual-sport participation policies that limit athletes to low-impact, non-competitive activities to mitigate injury risks that could disrupt gymnastics training and regional meet commitments [2]. In contrast, the USA-IGC program allows unrestricted dual-sport participation, framing cross-training as a benefit to overall athletic development, and mandates a more flexible 3–4 day weekly training schedule (9–12 total hours) for Level 5 travel team athletes [3]. Gold Medal’s travel teams for both tracks include regional meet travel, personalized skill refinement for Level 5’s mandatory optional skills, and monthly performance evaluations to track progression toward higher optional levels (Levels 6–10) [2].

## References
[1] USA Gymnastics. "2024-2028 Gymnastics Program Rules: Optional Levels Overview." USA Gymnastics Official Website, 2024.
[2] Gold Medal Gymnastics & Ninja. "Competitive Travel Team Guidelines for Level 5 Athletes." Gold Medal Gymnastics & Ninja Official Website, 2024.
[3] USA-IGC. "Program Policies for Optional Level Athletes: Dual-Sport and Training Requirements." USA-IGC Official Website, 2024.
rounds.judge_reasoning: Agent A correctly identifies Level 6 as the entry point to Optional competition in the USAG structure. Agent B incorrectly identifies Level 5 (which is a Compulsory level). Since Agent B failed the core entity identification (Deep Logic), Agent A is the clear winner. Agent A also correctly distinguishes the training commitments and policies for the specific gym requested.
